Experience
Senior Network Security Analyst On-Site
Accenture July 2018 – Present, Cairo, Egypt
•Install, configure, upgrade, and troubleshoot Cisco ASA firewalls (5500-X), including single and multi-context fire-
walls.
•Manage F5 LTM configuration (VIP , pools), SSL offloading, certificate renewals, code upgrade, and troubleshoot
related issues.
•Administer Pulse Secure (Juniper), including creating Roles/Realm, policies for remote access, reverse proxy, and
code upgrades.
•Implement PAC file configurations and prepare design documents (HLD, LLD), IP Schema, and configurations for
network devices in data center environments.
•Proficient in Incident Management, Change Management, and Problem Management following ITIL processes.
•Utilized Cisco Security Manager 4.x for efficient network security management.
Network Security Engineer On-Site
Zensar Technologies February 2016 – June 2018, Giza, Egypt
•Installed and upgraded Checkpoint firewalls (GAIA R75, R77) and managed policy deployments.
•Troubleshot Checkpoint firewalls, Palo Alto firewalls, and Cisco ASA’s for multiple sites across US, Canada, and
EMEA regions.
•Configured, upgraded, and troubleshot Cisco Routers (29xx, 28xx, 19xx), Cisco switches (2960, 3560), and Cisco
Meraki AP in Meraki Cloud.
•Performed F5 LTM configuration and resolved operational issues.
Network Engineer On-Site
Infosys Technologies May 2014 – January 2016, Alexandria, Egypt
•Installed, configured, upgraded, and troubleshot Checkpoint firewalls, Cisco Routers, and Switches.
•Configured, monitored, and troubleshot issues within organization LAN, WAN, and customer connectivity net-
works.
•Interfaced with vendors and service providers to ensure efficient resolution of hardware or software problems with
minimal downtime.
Network Security Administrator On-Site
HCL-Comnet November 2012 – April 2014, Cairo, Egypt
•Managed event monitoring and administration of RSA enVision 4.1 SIEM.
•Proficient in researching traffic patterns to identify false-positives and/or malicious traffic within IDS, IPS, scanners
(Qualys), and firewalls.
•Experienced in writing correlation rules, parsers, and developing UDS for unsupported device logs.
•Analyzed a large volume of security events while maintaining a high quality of analysis.
•Gained exposure to Change Configuration Management processes.
Junior Network Administrator On-Site
Wipro Technologies February 2010 – October 2012, Giza, Egypt
•Administered, managed, and troubleshot multi-vendor network infrastructure

including Cisco (Router 3845, switches
3750E, 2960, 4500 chassis, 5550/20 ASA firewalls) and Juniper Platforms (SA 6500 SSL VPN).
•Provisioned and troubleshot access issues related to RSA and Entrust (2FA) User Accounts.
•Executed weekly and monthly backups of all network and security device configurations.

Projects
Resiliency Project - Firewall Policy Lockdown Within Accenture
•Worked on resiliency project including firewall policy lockdown to enhance network security posture.
•Executed software code upgrades on all firewalls and load balancers, ensuring system stability and performance.
•Provided end-to-end coordination for major firewall deployment initiatives.
Skills
Firewall Technologies: Cisco ASA - Checkpoint (GAIA, Splat) - Palo Alto - F5 Load Balancer (LTM)
Firewall Management: Cisco Security Manager (CSM) - Checkpoint SmartCenter - Palo Alto Panorama

UDS Development - Pulse Secure (Juniper)
Tools & Processes: BMC Remedy - Service-now - ITIL (Incident, Change, Problem Management)
Programming/Scripting: Python - VB Scripting
Omar Salah is a Senior Network Security Analyst with contact information including email, phone number, GitHub, LinkedIn, and a personal website.
Omar Salah received a Bachelor of Engineering in Information Technology from Mumbai University between January 2006 and January 2010, located in Mumbai, Maharashtra.

From July 2018 to the present, Omar Salah worked as a Senior Network Security Analyst at Accenture in Cairo, Egypt, where he managed Cisco ASA firewalls, F5 LTM configurations, Pulse Secure, PAC file configurations, and network devices, and followed ITIL processes.
The infrastructure includes Cisco routers (3845), switches (3750E, 2960, 4500 chassis), and ASA firewalls (5550/20), along with Juniper SA 6500 SSL VPN platforms.
Access issues related to RSA and Entrust (2FA) User Accounts were provisioned and troubleshot.
Weekly and monthly backups of all network and security device configurations were executed.
Work was performed on a resiliency project, including firewall policy lockdown within Accenture, to enhance network security posture.
Software code upgrades were executed on all firewalls and load balancers to ensure system stability and performance.
End-to-end coordination was provided for major firewall deployment initiatives.
Skills include various firewall technologies like Cisco ASA, Checkpoint (GAIA, Splat), Palo Alto, and F5 Load Balancer (LTM).
Firewall management skills include Cisco Security Manager (CSM), Checkpoint SmartCenter, and Palo Alto Panorama.
Networking skills include Cisco Routers, Cisco Switches, Cisco Meraki AP, LAN/WAN, and Data Center Environments.
Security management skills include RSA Envision 4.1 (SIEM), IDS/IPS, Qualys Scanners, Correlation Rules, Parsers, UDS Development, and Pulse Secure (Juniper).
Tools & Processes include BMC Remedy, Service-now, and ITIL (Incident, Change, Problem Management).
Programming/Scripting skills include Python and VB Scripting.

Between February 2016 and June 2018, Omar Salah was a Network Security Engineer at Zensar Technologies in Giza, Egypt, where he managed Checkpoint and Palo Alto firewalls, Cisco routers and switches, and F5 LTM configurations across US, Canada, and EMEA regions.

From May 2014 to January 2016, Omar Salah worked as a Network Engineer at Infosys Technologies in Alexandria, Egypt, where he managed Checkpoint firewalls, Cisco Routers, and Switches, and interfaced with vendors.

Between November 2012 and April 2014, Omar Salah was a Network Security Administrator at HCL-Comnet in Cairo, Egypt, where he managed RSA enVision SIEM, researched traffic patterns, and wrote correlation rules.
From February 2010 to October 2012, Omar Salah was a Junior Network Administrator at Wipro Technologies in Giza, Egypt, where he administered and troubleshot multi-vendor network infrastructure.

Egypt, a land steeped in ancient history, also quietly serves as a modern hub for global IT operations. Across its major cities – Cairo, Giza, and Alexandria – a skilled workforce diligently maintains the digital infrastructure supporting international networks. This is exemplified by professionals like Omar Salah, a veteran of network security who has spent his career navigating the complexities of cybersecurity within the Egyptian landscape. 

Salah’s trajectory reflects Egypt’s role as a key location for outsourcing and IT service delivery. Beginning as a Junior Network Administrator in Giza with Wipro in 2010, he quickly ascended through roles demanding increasing responsibility and expertise. He’s become proficient in a wide array of technologies – Cisco, Checkpoint, Palo Alto, F5, Pulse Secure – managing firewalls, routers, switches, and security information and event management (SIEM) systems like RSA enVision. 

His experience isn’t confined to domestic networks. While based in Egypt, Salah has managed and secured networks spanning the US, Canada, and EMEA regions, working for companies like Zensar Technologies and Infosys. Currently, as a Senior Network Security Analyst at Accenture in Cairo, he leverages ITIL processes to ensure robust and reliable network security. Egypt, therefore, isn’t merely a geographical location in his story, but a strategic base from which he protects and optimizes global connectivity, a silent guardian of digital pathways. The country provides a fertile ground for cultivating talent capable of handling sophisticated network challenges on an international scale.

Accenture is a global professional services company, a quiet giant operating behind the scenes of many modern enterprises. Though rarely a household name, its influence is pervasive, particularly in the realm of network security. Operating with a significant presence in Cairo, Egypt, Accenture tackles complex challenges for its clients, often involving the hardening of critical infrastructure. Recent work includes a focused resiliency project – a meticulous lockdown of firewall policies designed to fortify network defenses. This isn’t simply about installing software; it’s about a deep understanding of network architecture, exemplified by professionals like Omar Salah, a Senior Network Security Analyst who has dedicated the last five years to Accenture’s operations. Salah’s expertise encompasses the intricate management of Cisco ASA firewalls, the load-balancing finesse of F5 LTM, secure remote access via Pulse Secure, and the precise control of PAC file configurations – all orchestrated within the framework of established ITIL processes. Accenture, therefore, isn’t just a service provider; it’s a meticulous architect of digital resilience, a place where technical expertise translates into tangible security for its clients.

Pulse Secure, now a part of Juniper Networks, is a critical component in the modern security landscape – a silent guardian of network access. It’s not a flashy face, but a robust and reliable system, often found working behind the scenes to ensure only authorized users reach sensitive data. Think of it as a meticulous gatekeeper, verifying identities and controlling access with a firm, yet adaptable hand. 

Originally built as a standalone security solution, Pulse Secure specializes in Secure Access Service Edge (SASE) architecture, offering VPN, network access control, and cloud security features. It’s a system favored by organizations needing to extend secure network access to remote workers and branch offices, providing a consistent security posture regardless of location. 

While capable of standing alone, Pulse Secure now benefits from the broader ecosystem and innovation of Juniper Networks, integrating seamlessly with their other security offerings. It’s a tool frequently managed by seasoned network security analysts like Omar Salah, who expertly configures and maintains its functionality alongside firewalls and load balancers, ensuring a layered defense against evolving cyber threats. Its strength lies in its detailed configuration options – from crafting precise correlation rules and parsers to developing User Defined Signatures – allowing security professionals to tailor its protection to specific organizational needs. It’s a system demanding expertise, but rewarding those who master its intricacies with a powerfully secure network.

Cisco Switches are the workhorses of modern networks, essential components in building robust and scalable connectivity. They aren’t glamorous, but they are relentlessly reliable, directing the flow of data within Local Area Networks (LANs), Wide Area Networks (WANs), and the demanding environments of Data Centers. Often found alongside their sibling, Cisco Routers, they form the backbone of communication, ensuring packets reach their destination with speed and efficiency. 

These switches aren’t simply boxes; they are configurable platforms, demanding a skilled hand for optimal performance. Network engineers like Omar Salah, who honed his expertise at both Zensar Technologies and Infosys Technologies, understand their intricacies. Salah’s experience demonstrates their critical role in geographically diverse networks – from the US and Canada to EMEA – where he directly managed and maintained Cisco Switches, ensuring seamless operation. They are a key element in a network professional’s toolkit, requiring a deep understanding of networking principles to deploy, troubleshoot, and secure. While often integrated with more automated systems like Cisco Meraki APs, the fundamental importance of a well-configured Cisco Switch remains constant, a silent facilitator of the digital world.

Cisco Routers are the workhorses of network infrastructure, essential components in the complex systems that connect the digital world. They aren’t flashy, but relentlessly reliable, directing data packets with precision across Local Area Networks (LANs), Wide Area Networks (WANs), and within the demanding environments of modern Data Centers. Often found alongside their sibling, Cisco Switches, and increasingly complemented by wireless solutions like Cisco Meraki APs, these routers form the backbone of connectivity for organizations large and small. 

They demand a specific skillset to manage – a knowledge base honed by engineers like Omar Salah, who spent years navigating their configurations at both Zensar Technologies and Infosys Technologies. Salah’s expertise extended across continents, from the US and Canada to EMEA, ensuring seamless data flow through these vital devices. He didn’t just *use* Cisco Routers; he *managed* them, a testament to their critical role in maintaining network security and performance. His work involved not only direct configuration but also vendor interaction, highlighting the routers’ place within a broader ecosystem of networking technology. They are a foundational element, requiring dedicated professionals to keep the digital arteries flowing.

Omar Salah is a seasoned network security professional, currently a Senior Network Security Analyst at Accenture in Cairo. A graduate of Mumbai University’s Information Technology program (2006-2010), his career has been a steady ascent through increasingly complex network environments. He’s a hands-on expert, deeply familiar with the intricacies of securing modern networks – a skillset honed over years managing critical infrastructure. 

Salah’s expertise centers around firewall technologies; he’s proficient with Cisco ASA, Checkpoint, and Palo Alto systems, alongside load balancing configurations using F5 LTM and secure remote access solutions like Pulse Secure. He’s equally comfortable with the foundational elements of network infrastructure, possessing strong experience administering Cisco routers and switches. 

His journey began at Wipro Technologies in 2010, where he cut his teeth troubleshooting multi-vendor networks. He quickly progressed, taking on security responsibilities at HCL-Comnet, where he delved into security information and event management (SIEM) with RSA enVision, learning to identify and respond to threats through traffic analysis and correlation rule creation. Subsequent roles at Infosys Technologies and Zensar Technologies broadened his scope, managing networks spanning the US, Canada, and EMEA regions. Throughout his career, he’s demonstrated a commitment to best practices, consistently adhering to ITIL processes. He maintains an active online presence, accessible via email, phone, GitHub, LinkedIn, and a personal website.

ITIL is a quietly powerful framework, a set of best practices for managing IT services. Not a tool itself, but a guiding philosophy, it’s the unseen hand shaping how organizations respond to disruptions and proactively improve. Think of it as the meticulous blueprint for keeping the digital world running smoothly. At its heart lie three core disciplines: Incident Management – the rapid response team extinguishing fires as they flare up; Change Management – the careful architects planning and executing upgrades with minimal disruption; and Problem Management – the detectives digging beneath the surface to prevent those fires from starting in the first place. 

Individuals like Omar Salah, a Senior Network Security Analyst at Accenture, operate *within* this framework, leveraging ITIL processes to maintain network stability and security. He doesn’t simply configure firewalls and load balancers; he does so *according to* ITIL principles, ensuring changes are controlled, incidents are logged and resolved, and underlying issues are systematically addressed. 

While tools like BMC Remedy and ServiceNow provide the platforms for enacting ITIL, the framework itself is the enduring structure. It’s a constantly evolving set of guidelines, adaptable to diverse environments, but always focused on delivering value and minimizing risk in the complex world of IT service delivery. It’s less about *what* technology is used, and more about *how* it’s managed – a commitment to order amidst chaos, and a dedication to continuous improvement.
